The Art of Sourdough

By Jane Baker

Sourdough bread is leavened not by commercial yeast but by a living culture of wild yeast and lactic-acid bacteria. That culture, kept alive by regular feedings of flour and water, is what gives a good loaf its springy crumb and its gently sour flavour.

The process begins with the starter. A healthy starter doubles in size within a few hours of feeding, smells pleasantly tangy, and floats when a spoonful is dropped into water. Bakers who skip this readiness test often blame the recipe when the real culprit is an under-active culture.

Once the dough is mixed, time does most of the work. A long, cool fermentation develops flavour and strengthens the gluten network without aggressive kneading. Patience, more than technique, separates a flat brick from an airy, open crumb.

Finally, a hot oven and a burst of steam give the loaf its crust. The steam keeps the surface flexible long enough for the bread to expand before the crust sets, which is why a covered pot produces such a dramatic rise at home.
